paper.title: Synthetic Lethal Interaction between Oncogenic KRAS Dependency and STK33 Suppression in Human Cancer Cells

claims.text: STK33 suppression selectively kills KRAS-dependent (mutant KRAS-driven) human cancer cells but not KRAS-independent cells, revealing a synthetic lethal interaction between STK33 and oncogenic KRAS dependency.

loci.quote: we identify a member of the serine/threonine protein kinase family, STK33, as an essential gene selectively required for survival of KRAS mutant-dependent cancer cells

claims.text: STK33's role in maintaining KRAS-dependent cancer cell survival is kinase-activity dependent but functions independently of the canonical RAF-MEK-ERK and PI3K-AKT effector pathways downstream of KRAS.

loci.quote: this STK33 requirement is dependent on its kinase activity but is independent of its role in KRAS-mediated transformation and of the RAF-MEK-ERK and PI3K-AKT effector pathways

claims.text: STK33 promotes survival of KRAS-dependent cells by regulating suppression of mitochondrial apoptosis, mechanistically through modulation of S6K1-mediated inactivation of the death agonist BAD at Ser112.

loci.quote: Suppression of STK33 function leads to differential regulation of markers for mitochondrial outer membrane polarization and apoptosis specifically in KRAS mutant-dependent cells th

claims.text: A large-scale RNAi screen using shRNA constructs targeting several hundred genes across ~30 KRAS mutant and wild-type cancer cell lines identified STK33 as a gene whose knockdown produces a viability effect that correlates specifically with KRAS mutation status/dependency.

loci.quote: We conducted a request-based shRNA screen in a genetically diverse panel of cancer cell lines to identify genes that are selectively required for the survival of cells bearing muta

claims.text: The synthetic lethal effect of STK33 suppression is dependent on the cell's degree of oncogenic KRAS dependency (i.e., KRAS-dependent lines are sensitive, KRAS-independent lines with KRAS mutations are not), rather than merely on KRAS mutational status per se.

loci.quote: the requirement for STK33 correlates with the state of KRAS dependency rather than with the mere presence of KRAS mutations

claims.text: Pharmacological or genetic inhibition of STK33 kinase activity selectively induces death in KRAS-dependent cancer cells, supporting STK33 as a potential therapeutic target in KRAS mutant cancers that have been refractory to direct RAS-pathway drug targeting.

loci.quote: expression of a kinase-dead STK33 mutant fails to rescue the effects of STK33 suppression, indicating that its kinase activity is required for maintenance of the survival of KRAS m

retrieved.title: Synthetic Lethal Interaction between Oncogenic KRAS Dependency and STK33 Suppression in Human Cancer Cells
retrieved.abstract: An alternative to therapeutic targeting of oncogenes is to perform "synthetic lethality" screens for genes that are essential only in the context of specific cancer-causing mutations. We used high-throughput RNA interference (RNAi) to identify synthetic lethal interactions in cancer cells harboring mutant KRAS, the most commonly mutated human oncogene. We find that cells that are dependent on mutant KRAS exhibit sensitivity to suppression of the serine/threonine kinase STK33 irrespective of tissue origin, whereas STK33 is not required by KRAS-independent cells. STK33 promotes cancer cell viability in a kinase activity-dependent manner by regulating the suppression of mitochondrial apoptosis mediated through S6K1-induced inactivation of the death agonist BAD selectively in mutant KRAS-dependent cells. These observations identify STK33 as a target for treatment of mutant KRAS-driven cancers and demonstrate the potential of RNAi screens for discovering functional dependencies created by oncogenic mutations that may enable therapeutic intervention for cancers with "undruggable" genetic alterations.
retrieved.rationale: This is the primary source paper describing the discovery of the synthetic lethal interaction between STK33 suppression and KRAS dependency. It directly demonstrates that KRAS-dependent cells are sensitive to STK33 suppression while KRAS-independent cells are not, and provides mechanistic insight through S6K1 and BAD signaling.

retrieved.title: Faculty Opinions recommendation of Synthetic lethal interaction between oncogenic KRAS dependency and STK33 suppression in human cancer cells.
retrieved.venue: Faculty Opinions – Post-Publication Peer Review of the Biomedical Literature
retrieved.rationale: Faculty Opinions recommendation of the primary STK33-KRAS synthetic lethality paper. Supports the finding but is a commentary/endorsement rather than independent experimental evidence.

retrieved.title: High-Throughput RNA Interference Screening Identifies Synthetic Lethality Between Oncogenic KRAS Dependency and Suppression of STK33
retrieved.abstract: Abstract Activating RAS mutations are among the most common pathogenetic events in a broad spectrum of hematologic malignancies and epithelial tumors. However, oncogenic RAS has thus far not proven to be a tractable target for therapeutic intervention. An alternative to direct targeting of known oncogenes is to perform “synthetic lethality” screens to identify genes that are selectively required for cell viability in the context of specific cancer-causing mutations. Using this approach, we have discovered a synthetic lethal interaction between mutant KRAS, the most frequently mutated oncogene in human cancer, and inactivation of the gene encoding the STK33 serine/threonine protein kinase. To identify genes that are essential for cell viability in the context of mutant KRAS, we performed high-throughput loss-of-function RNA interference (RNAi) screens in eight human cancer cell lines (mutant KRAS, n=4; wildtype KRAS, n=4), representing seven different tumor types (acute myeloid leukemia, multiple myeloma, colon cancer, breast cancer, ovarian cancer, prostate cancer, glioblastoma), as well as normal human fibroblasts and mammary epithelial cells. We screened each cell line with a sub…
retrieved.rationale: Appears to be an early presentation or abstract of the synthetic lethal screening results identifying STK33-KRAS interaction. Describes the high-throughput RNAi screening methodology across multiple cancer cell types (mutant KRAS vs wildtype KRAS), directly supporting the claim.

retrieved.title: Synthetic Lethal Vulnerabilities in KRAS-Mutant Cancers.
retrieved.rationale: Comprehensive review of synthetic lethal vulnerabilities in KRAS-mutant cancers. Likely includes discussion of STK33 as a synthetic lethal partner, providing independent endorsement of the concept from literature review perspective.

topReasons.text: Genuine independent corroboration is essentially absent: of six 'supporting' works, five are the primary paper itself, its own prior conference abstract, or Faculty Opinions endorsements; only one independent item (a narrative review) is present, and it is not a replication.
The primary paper is high-impact, open access, and mechanistically detailed (S6K1/BAD axis), which lends internal plausibility and scrutinizability.

verdicts.reasoning:
The claim asserts a synthetic lethal interaction between STK33 suppression and mutant-KRAS dependency, discovered by Scholl et al. (Cell 2009) via high-throughput RNAi. The mechanism (STK33 → S6K1 → BAD → mitochondrial apoptosis, selective in KRAS-dependent cells) is specific and biologically plausible. The corroboration summary looks favorable at face value: 461 citations, 6 supporting works, 0 contradicting, not retracted. However, the supplied 'supporting' items are heavily non-independent: the primary paper itself, its own Blood 2008 conference abstract, and three Faculty Opinions commentaries, none constitute independent experimental replication. Only ONE genuinely independent work is listed (Aguirre & Hahn 2018 review), and it is a narrative review, not a replication attempt.

Critically, this claim is a landmark case in the reproducibility literature where the specific finding FAILED to replicate. The corroboration engine's '0 contradicting / 0 failed replications' is a retrieval gap, not evidence of robustness. In fact, multiple high-profile independent groups (e.g., Babij et al., Cancer Res 2011; Luo et al., Cell 2009 and follow-ups; and Amgen/independent teams) reported that although STK33 knockdown was confirmed to reduce viability, the effect was NOT selective for KRAS-mutant/dependent cells, and STK33 kinase activity was not required, directly undermining the synthetic-lethal selectivity that is the core of this claim. STK33 became a widely cited example of an RNAi screening hit that did not hold up, contributing to concerns about off-target RNAi effects. The claim also predates the RNAi-to-CRISPR transition, and large-scale dependency maps (DepMap/Project Achilles) did not recover STK33 as a selective KRAS dependency.

Given: (1) the core selectivity claim is contradicted by several independent groups not captured in the retrieved set, (2) genuine independent corroboration here is effectively nil, (3) the RNAi methodology of the era is prone to off-target artifacts, and (4) the field base rate for high-profile preclinical synthetic-lethal RNAi hits replicating is low, the replication likelihood is low. I place it below 0.25 but not at the floor, because the underlying viability effect of STK33 knockdown was reproduced by some; it is the KRAS-selectivity that failed. Uncertainty is moderate because the disconfirming literature was not directly supplied in the evidence set and I am reasoning from field knowledge and the one-sided/non-independent nature of the provided corroboration.

topReasons.text: Corroboration is largely non-independent: one primary source (2009 Cell) plus a same-author Blood abstract and Faculty Opinions endorsements; independent_supporting_works = 1 and it is a review, not a replication.
The claim is a strong negative mechanistic assertion (independence from RAF-MEK-ERK and PI3K-AKT) resting on 2009-era RNAi screens prone to off-target effects, making it intrinsically fragile.

verdicts.reasoning:
The claim asserts that STK33's support of KRAS-dependent cancer cell survival is (a) kinase-activity dependent and (b) independent of the canonical RAF-MEK-ERK and PI3K-AKT effector pathways downstream of KRAS. This originates from the 2009 Scholl et al. Cell paper (Barbie/Hahn lab), a high-profile synthetic lethality screen.

Mechanism/strength: The original paper proposed a specific, somewhat unusual mechanism, STK33 acting via S6K1/BAD to suppress mitochondrial apoptosis selectively in mutant-KRAS cells, distinct from the canonical MAPK/PI3K axes. This is a mechanistic claim requiring the perturbation to phenocopy across kinase-dead mutants and be dissociable from RAF-MEK-ERK/PI3K-AKT signaling.

The corroboration table shows paper_cited_by=461 but only 1 independent supporting work and the rest are the same authors, a Blood abstract of the same work, and Faculty Opinions endorsements (not independent replications). Critically, this specific finding is one of the most notorious failed replications in cancer preclinical biology: subsequent independent efforts, most prominently Babij et al. (Amgen, Cancer Research 2011) and later work by other groups, failed to reproduce the STK33-KRAS synthetic lethal dependency and could not confirm STK33 kinase-activity-dependent selective killing of KRAS-mutant cells. The STK33 story is frequently cited as a canonical example of an irreproducible synthetic-lethal hit. The retrieval set here does NOT surface those disconfirming works (a gap in the supplied evidence), but the known base rate for RNAi-based synthetic-lethal screen hits from this era is poor, and this particular claim is historically discredited.

Even setting aside my background knowledge, the intrinsic evidentiary support here is thin: a single primary source plus non-independent echoes, no design checks assessable, RNAi off-target concerns were rampant in 2009-era screens, and the mechanistic 'independence from RAF-MEK-ERK/PI3K-AKT' is a strong negative claim that is inherently hard to establish and easy to overturn. The one 'independent' review (Aguirre & Hahn) is co-authored by the original senior author's collaborator network and is a review, not a replication.

Given the well-documented replication failures of the STK33 synthetic-lethality finding and the compounded fragility of the specific kinase-dependent, pathway-independent mechanistic assertion, I assign a low replication likelihood. Uncertainty is moderate because the supplied retrieval set omits the direct contradicting works, but the base rate and history strongly favor non-replication.

topReasons.text: The claim derives entirely from the single primary source (Scholl et al. 2009 Cell); no independent experimental replication of the STK33-KRAS mechanistic dependency is present in the retrieved evidence.

Supporting 'corroboration' consists mostly of the original paper, its own abstract, and Faculty Opinions endorsements, not independent replications; only one nominally independent item and it is a review.

verdicts.reasoning: The claim is a specific mechanistic assertion: STK33 promotes survival of KRAS-dependent cells by suppressing mitochondrial apoptosis via S6K1-mediated inactivation of BAD at Ser112. This comes directly from the 2009 Scholl et al. Cell paper (Barbie/Hahn lab), which is the primary source. While that paper is highly cited (~461-567), the STK33 synthetic-lethality finding is one of the most notorious failed replications in cancer preclinical biology. Multiple independent groups, most prominently Babij et al. (Amgen, Cancer Research 2011) and Luo et al., were unable to reproduce the KRAS-selective dependency on STK33, and STK33 inhibitors failed to show the predicted KRAS-mutant-selective killing. The mechanistic sub-claim (S6K1→BAD Ser112) rests entirely on the original paper and inherits this replication failure. The corroboration record supplied here (0 contradicting works, 1 independent supporting review) is misleading: the retrieved set is dominated by the original paper, its own conference abstract, and Faculty Opinions commentaries, none independent experimental replications. The single "independent" item is a review, not a replication. The deterministic checks are all uninformative (na). Given the well-documented literature of failed independent replication of the STK33-KRAS synthetic lethality, and that this mechanistic detail is even more specific and thus more fragile, replication likelihood is low. I retain moderate uncertainty because the corroboration data as supplied does not flag the contradictions, but domain knowledge strongly weighs against replication.

topReasons.text: Corroboration is dominated by non-independent sources (the primary paper, its own conference abstract by the same authors, and Faculty Opinions commentaries); only one independent work exists and it is a review, not a replication.
The claim accurately describes the original high-throughput RNAi screen and its reported result, which is genuine as an initial observation even if the KRAS-specificity did not hold.

verdicts.reasoning:
The claim describes the Scholl et al. (2009, Cell) finding that an RNAi/shRNA synthetic-lethality screen across a panel of KRAS mutant and wild-type cancer lines identified STK33 as selectively required in KRAS-dependent cells. As a description of what the original screen reported, the claim is an accurate account of the primary paper and its associated conference abstract (Blood 2008). The retrieved corroboration, however, is almost entirely non-independent: it consists of the primary paper itself, its Blood abstract by the same authors, three Faculty Opinions commentaries endorsing the same paper, and one later review. Only one work is flagged as independent, and it is a review that discusses the concept rather than an independent replication.

Critically, this specific finding is one of the most notorious failed replications in cancer preclinical biology. Multiple well-powered independent studies published shortly after (most prominently Babij et al., Cancer Research 2011, from Amgen, and additional work) failed to reproduce the KRAS-selective dependency on STK33: STK33 knockdown/inhibition did not selectively kill KRAS-mutant lines, and the correlation between STK33 dependency and KRAS mutation status did not hold up. STK33 is frequently cited as a canonical example of an irreproducible synthetic-lethal hit. Although the corroboration table records zero contradicting works and zero failed replications, this reflects incomplete retrieval rather than an absence of disconfirming evidence; the true literature base is strongly one-sided against the KRAS-specificity claim.

The design checks are all "na" (no randomization, blinding, controls, or multiple-comparison correction assessable), which is neutral but offers no reassurance for a high-dimensional screen prone to false positives. Given the well-documented replication failures of exactly this correlational KRAS–STK33 dependency, the replication likelihood should be well below the base rate. I set it low but not extreme, acknowledging retrieval did not surface the contradicting studies directly and the original observation of an STK33 viability effect in some lines is real even if the KRAS-specific correlation is not.

topReasons.text: Retrieved corroboration is overwhelmingly non-independent: the source paper, its own conference abstract, and three Faculty Opinions commentaries all derive from the same authors/finding; only one true independent work (a review) supports it.
The original Cell paper is well-designed, open access, and highly cited, providing a coherent mechanism (S6K1/BAD) for the dependency-specific effect.

verdicts.reasoning:
The claim states that STK33 synthetic lethality tracks the state of KRAS dependency rather than mere KRAS mutational status. This is the central finding of the 2009 Scholl et al. Cell paper (Barbie/Hahn lab), a highly cited work (461-567 citations). The retrieved corroboration, however, is almost entirely non-independent: the primary paper, its conference-abstract precursor (same authors), and three Faculty Opinions commentaries all trace back to the same source. There is essentially only one independent work (a 2018 review) that endorses the concept, and reviews do not constitute independent experimental replication.

Critically, this specific claim is one of the most notorious failed replications in cancer preclinical biology. STK33 became a prominent example of the reproducibility crisis: multiple well-resourced independent groups (notably Babij et al., Cancer Res 2011, from Amgen; and Luo et al./others) attempted to reproduce the STK33-KRAS synthetic lethal dependency and failed to confirm that STK33 is selectively required in KRAS-mutant/KRAS-dependent cells. STK33 inhibitor programs did not bear out the dependency. This is widely cited in the reproducibility literature (e.g., Begley & Ellis type discussions) as a canonical non-replicating target.

The corroboration metadata records zero contradicting works and zero failed replications, but this reflects incomplete retrieval rather than a genuinely clean record, the disconfirming literature exists and is substantial. My domain knowledge of the strong, published independent contradictions outweighs the citation count, since high citation count here partly reflects the paper's fame as a replication-failure case study. The deterministic checks are all uninformative (na), providing no additional lift. Given credible independent disconfirmation of exactly this dependency claim, replication likelihood is low.

topReasons.text: Only one of the six 'supporting' works is genuinely independent, and it is a review that discusses the concept rather than an experimental replication; the rest are the primary paper, its own prior abstract, and Faculty Opinions commentaries.
The primary paper is a high-impact, mechanistically detailed Cell publication with kinase-dead rescue controls, giving the original finding internal plausibility.

verdicts.reasoning:
The claim asserts that STK33 kinase inhibition (pharmacological or genetic) selectively kills KRAS-dependent cancer cells, positioning STK33 as a synthetic-lethal therapeutic target in KRAS-mutant cancers. Mechanistically the original paper (Scholl et al., Cell 2009) proposed STK33 supports viability via S6K1-mediated BAD inactivation, with kinase-dead rescue failure supporting the kinase-activity dependence.

Weighing the evidence: The corroboration counts appear numerically supportive (paper_cited_by=461, 6 total supporting works, 0 contradicting). However, these are misleading. Nearly all of the "supporting" retrieved items are NOT independent, they are the primary paper itself, its earlier conference abstract (Blood 2008), Faculty Opinions endorsements (commentary, not experiments), and only ONE genuinely independent work (a 2018 review that merely discusses the concept). independent_supporting_works=1, and that single item is a review, not a replication attempt.

Critically, this specific finding is a well-known, high-profile REPLICATION FAILURE in cancer biology. Multiple independent groups (notably Babij et al., Cancer Research 2011, from Amgen; also work from Merck and others) attempted to reproduce the STK33-KRAS synthetic lethality and FAILED to confirm that STK33 kinase activity is selectively required for KRAS-dependent cell survival. The synthetic lethal relationship did not hold up, STK33 was found to be largely dispensable, and no viable STK33-targeted therapeutic emerged. This is a canonical example cited in reproducibility discussions. The corroboration dataset failed to capture these contradicting works (contradicting_works=0 is a retrieval gap, not genuine absence).

The deterministic checks are all "na" (design not assessable from abstract) and thus neutral; open-access passes but is minor. These do not move the estimate.

Given the strong, well-documented independent disconfirmation of this exact claim in the literature, and the fact that the apparent corroboration is almost entirely non-independent self-citation and commentary, replication likelihood is low. I set it at 0.2 with moderate uncertainty, since the retrieval set itself contains no contradicting works and I am reasoning partly from field knowledge of the failed replications.

sourceText: Synthetic Lethal Interaction between Oncogenic KRAS Dependency and STK33 Suppression in Human Cancer Cells

meta.tokensNote: Paper cached across calls; Opus for adjudication.

narrative:
This 2009 study proposed STK33 as a synthetic lethal target in KRAS-dependent cancer cells, meaning its suppression would selectively kill tumors driven by mutant KRAS. Litmus rates the paper fragile overall, with a reproducibility likelihood of just 21 percent (interval 10 to 32 percent). No individual checks were flagged as failing or as warnings, so the low score is driven by the low per-claim confidence rather than by any single detected defect. The central claim, that STK33 suppression selectively kills KRAS-dependent cells, sits at 20 percent. The mechanistic claim that STK33 acts through kinase activity but independently of the RAF-MEK-ERK and PI3K-AKT pathways is rated unsupported at 14 percent, the weakest link in the chain. The BAD/S6K1 apoptosis mechanism and the therapeutic-target claim are likewise fragile at 20 percent each. The strongest element is the RNAi screen that first surfaced STK33, at 31 percent, but even that falls short of confidence.

The transparency picture is thin. The only positive signal is that the paper is open access, which aids inspection but says nothing about whether the underlying data or reagents support the conclusions. There are no listed shortcomings from the check battery, yet the absence of stronger transparency signals, combined with mechanistic claims that Litmus could not corroborate, keeps confidence low. The mechanistic story is the shakiest part: the finding rests on a specific pathway model (kinase-dependent, effector-pathway-independent, routed through BAD inactivation) that the audit rates as unsupported to fragile.

The literature context is worth reading carefully. All six sources queried were coded as supporting, with zero contradicting and zero failed replications recorded in the fact sheet. That may look reassuring, but it does not lift the verdict above fragile, and this case is well known in the field for a reason the audit does not capture in its literature tally: STK33 as a KRAS synthetic lethal target became a prominent example of a high-profile preclinical finding that later resisted independent replication. Given that history and the low internal confidence scores here, treat the therapeutic-target conclusion as a hypothesis rather than an established result. If you intend to build on this, prioritize independent replication of the core selective-killing effect and the kinase-dependence and BAD mechanism before committing resources, and do not assume the effector-pathway-independence claim holds.

manifest.citation: Litmus reproducibility audit of Scholl et al., "Synthetic Lethal Interaction between Oncogenic KRAS Dependency and STK33 Suppression in Human Cancer Cells" (doi:10.1016/j.cell.2009.03.017). Verdict: fragile 21%. Litmus engine 0.2.0 [a3b80008], 2026-07-12. Audit lpq9wt-q.
